EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak F1 tornado touched down just northeast of Wright Basin.  Numerous trees were blown down and several homes were damaged.  One home had most of its roof taken off.  Numerous boathouses were destroyed with several boats torn from their moorings.  The tornado then skipped northeast and damaged two hangars at Milton airport.  Two ultra light planes inside one of the hangars suffered damage.  The tornado continued northeast and again touched down along Stagecoach Road.  A chain-link fence and several trees were blown down.  A travel trailer was moved three feet with pots and pans inside the trailer sucked out of the cabinets and thrown onto the floor.  The tornado then blew shingles from a couple of homes as it moved northeast.  Just before lifting back into the cloud, the tornado flipped two vehicles parked at a home.  The first, a pickup truck, was flipped two times and came to rest in a field on its wheels.  The second, a Lincoln Continental,  was  flipped over on its top and came to rest against a tree.  Both vehicles were destroyed.  A couple of residents referred to the tornado sound as a sonic boom.

Five homes suffered minor to moderate roof and structural damage, along with major porch damage, on Indian Boulevard on the north side of Lake Charlotte from thunderstorm downburst winds.  Thunderstorm winds also damaged trees and potted plants on Orange Blossom Boulevard near State Road 66.

Thunderstorm downburst winds estimated at 50 to 55 mph caused major roof damage on the south side of a warehouse located on Riverview Drive west of Oak Street.  The design and lightweight material used for the roof and overhang were significant contributors to the amount of damage incurred from the downburst wind.A worker observed straight winds, blowing from south to north at the leading edge of a gust front, impact the approximately 75 foot wide south side and fifteen foot high roof overhang of the damaged warehouse. The wind lifted the lightweight aluminum overhang upward and peeled it back along with an additional 20 feet of light-weight roofing material.

Two males were struck and injured by lightning, one fatal, while at a golfing range near the intersection of Westshore Blvd. and West Columbus Drive.  Eyewitnesses reported the golfers were struck by the first observed lightning bolt that emanated from the cirrus anvil of a distant thunderstorm.  The sun was shining at the time of the lightning strike.The two men were standing ten feet apart when the lightning bolt struck.  The bolt struck the graphite club of the fatally injured golfer and passed through his body to the other severely injured golfer. Witnesses stated that both golfers were thrown several feet into the air when the bolt struck.  The non-fatally injured suffered chest and facial burns from the lightning strike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail, driven by a 60 mph northeast wind, combined with a gentle northeast to southwest sloping terrain, and falling in torrential rain of four inches in less than 30 minutes, allowed hail to accumulate to depths of up to four feet in shallow ravines and low-lying areas in the hill country of eastern Pasco county.Most of the accumulated hail occurred one-quarter mile west of U.S. Highway 301 and Bailey Hill Road intersection, 2.5 miles north of Zephyrhills.

An intense microburst struck a hardware store in the Ensley area near the intersections of US Highway 90 and US Highway 29.  Estimated wind speed of the microburst was 90 mph.  Most of the roof was peeled from the building.  After the wind got inside the building through the roof it caused the inside of the building to bow out on three sides.  One side of the concrete block building partially collapsed.  Insulation and debris were blown in a fan-like pattern for about 200 yards south through west through northwest of the microburst.  Several people reported seeing what looked like a funnel cloud moving along above the tree tops before the wind damaged the building.  Nothing else in the area was damaged.

Lightning was blamed for a house fire along Santa Rosa Sound in the Navarre area.  Lightning evidently hit near a utility room.  A fire started in the utility room and spread up along a wall onto a portion of the roof.  It took firefighters almost an hour to extinguish the blaze.  No injuries were reported.
Lightning struck the Harold Assembly of God Church and caused a small fire.  A woman getting out of her car near the church felt the effects of the lightning, but did not require medical treatment.

Three vehicles suffered major water damage at the low-lying intersection while a few mobile homes at an adjacent mobile home park received minor water damage.Three to four secondary and gravel roads near the U.S. Highway 301 and Bailey Hill Road intersection were washed out from the heavy rains.

A hard freeze produced temperatures that dropped into the low to middle 20's across most of West Central Florida and Southwest Florida for durations of up to six to eight hours.  Freeze damage to crops over the region was estimated to be 12.9 million dollars.  Nearly 90 percent of Southwest Florida's winter crop was severely damaged by the freeze.Primary crops severely damaged by the freeze were beans, squash, tomatoes, sweet corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant, cucumbers, cabbage, watermelons and strawberries.  Exotic fruit and plants were also damaged by the freeze, primarily over Southwest Florida.Crop losses in Lee county were reported at $9.8 million dollars, Charlotte county reported $2.2 million dollars, Highlands county $583,000 dollars, and $294,000 dollars.  Freeze damage to other counties inWest Central and Southwest Florida was minimal.Citrus damage was concentrated over Lee, Charlotte, De Soto, Hardee, Sarasota, Manatee, Highlands and Polk counties in descending order.  Lee county reported citrus fruit losses of nearly 50 percent while Charlotte county reported a 45 percent loss.  Citrus fruit damage in De Soto county was estimated at 35 percent, Sarasota and Manatee counties 30 percent, Hardee county 25 percent, while Highlands and Polk counties estimated nearly 20 percent.  Wood damage to citrus trees in De Soto county was reported as serious while Highlands county reported moderate to heavy damage.  Freeze damage to the wood of citrus trees, to a lessor extent, was also reported in Hardee, Sarasota, Manatee, Charlotte and Lee counties.The freeze also caused a major power outage in the city of Arcadia in Hardee county where nearly 7,000residents were without electricity for at least eight hours.

A small warehouse incurred roof damage and a gazebo was picked up and destroyed by the tornadic wind near Giddens Street and 15th Avenue. A woman working in the damaged warehouse received neck and back injuries from the wind blown roof debris.The tornado crossed the intersection of Hillsborough Avenue and 17th Street where it blew down the sign of a Checker Hamburgers fast-food restaurant, damaged several dozen vehicles of an auto dealership and downed a large flag pole.The next touchdown occurred on 30th Street and Crawford where two ten inch diameter pine trees were snapped off twenty feet above ground level.  The final touchdown occurred at a water treatment plant along the Hillsborough River where minor damage was reported.

A strong F2 tornado touched down in the Lake Region mobile home park along U.S. Highway 27 and County Road 544 in Polk county.  Several mobile homes sustained major to severe damage.  Residents of Haines City who heard the tornado described that the sound resembled a high-pitched whine.The F2 tornado moved northeast and travelled across U.S. Highway 27 and caused significant wall and roof damage to several well built wood, mixed brick and mobile homes over mainly south and east Haines City from County Road 544 northeast to County Road 580.  The Boone Middle School over eastern Haines City had heavy damage to a main building while eleven portable classrooms were destroyed or heavily damaged.Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped half way up from the base.  Several orange trees were sheared near the base along County Road 544.  Several power poles were snapped and power lines downed along the path of the F2 tornado.Total damage was estimated at six million dollars.  Seventy-five homes were destroyed, another 75-100 suffered major damage while another 75-100 received minor damage.  Most of the homes destroyed were mobile or pre-fabricated homes.  Also, at least ten vehicles were damaged or destroyed by the tornado.Nearly 2,000 electrical customers were without power from power poles and lines blown down by the tornadic wind.Most injuries that occurred during the tornado touchdown resulted from wind blown debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Myakka River at Myakka State Park crested at 9.4 feet on the 16th, nearly two and a half feet above the flood stage of seven feet.  Road flooding from the crest of the Myakka River occurred along Brentwood Drive and Dixon Roads.
The Peace River along U.S. Highway 17 in Zolfo Springs crested at 16.8 feet, near a foot above the flood stage of 16 feet, on the 15th.

The Peace River along State Road 70 in Arcadia crested at 14.7 feet, nearly three and a half feet above the flood stage of eleven feet, on the 18th.  Six homes along the banks of the Peace River received minor flood damage.

The Alafia River along State Road 640 in Lithia crested at 17.5 feet, nearly four and a half feet above the flood stage of 13 feet, on the 28th.  Sixty mobile homes and rental properties were damaged for a second time in a month along the Alafia River.

In Hillsborough county, heavy rains caused the Little Manatee River to crest at 14.7 feet, over three and a half feet above the eleven foot flood stage at U.S. Highway 301, on the 28th.  A few homes were damaged by floodwaters in Ruskin.
The Peace River along State Road 70 in Arcadia crested at 11.8 feet, nearly a foot above the flood stage of eleven feet, on the 31st.

The Hillsborough River in Hillsborough county crested at 34.0 feet at Morris Bridge Road, nearly two feet above the flood stage of 32 feet, on the 29th.  Nearly 30 homes incurred minor damage from floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding to homes along and near the Peace River along U.S. Highway 17 near the Hardee-De Soto county line.  The Horse Creek in Hardee county also overflowed it's banks and caused minor to moderate water damage to homes and businesses.  Damage in Hardee county from the flooding was estimated at $250,000.In Pinellas county, heavy rainfall caused flooding in two mobile home parks in Largo.  The heavy rainfall seriously damaged road infrastructure along Curlew Road from County Road 1 to Belcher Road. Flooding of homes and roads also occurred in the Tarpon Woods area along Brooker Creek in Pinellas county.  Damage in Pinellas county from the flood was estimated at $500,000.In Polk county, five to seven inches of rain fell from Lakeland south to Bartow and east to Auburndale and caused extensive road and infrastructure damage.  Most of the damage occurred in the Green Swamp, along Spirit Lake Road, Misty Lake Road, Thornhill Road and Red Hawk Drive where eight to ten homes were flooded with six to twelve inches of water with isolated pockets up to four feet deep.  Damage from the flood in Polk county was estimated at $750,000.In Sarasota county, six to eight inches of rain in less than twelve hours caused road and home flooding along Lockwood Ridge Road near Bahia Vista Street in Sarasota.  Damage from the flood in Sarasota county was estimated $80,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hillsborough River at Hillsborough State Park crested at 13.5 feet, 3.5 feet above the flood stage of ten feet on the 14th.  Several homes in a mobile home park, along State Road 579 near U.S. Highway 301, received minor to moderate damage.

The Cypress Creek at Worthington Gardens along State Road 54 crested at 10.9 feet, nearly three feet above the flood stage of eight feet on the 17th.  Minor flooding and water damage occurred at a fish and trailer camp along State Road 54.

The Hillsborough River in Hillsborough county crested at 34.3 feet at Morris Bridge Road on the 14th and caused damage to 30 to 40 homes and apartments along its banks between Fletcher and Fowler Roads.
The Manatee River at Myakka Head along State Road 64 crested at 7.4 feet on the 5th, nearly a half foot above the flood stage of seven feet.  Several tomato fields were flooded by crest.
The Myakka River at Myakka State Park crested at 7.2 feet on the 6th, a few inches above the flood stage of seven feet.
Thunderstorm winds caused minor siding and lanai damage to a few mobile homes in Bradenton.

The Withlacoochee River at Croom crested at 10.9 feet, nearly two feet above the flood stage of nine feet on the 31st.  Several homes along Hernando-Pasco county line, from Croom south to Ridge Manor, were damaged by floodwaters.  The most heavily damaged areas were near County Road 575 and U.S. Highway 301 at the Talisman Estates along the eastern Pasco-Hernando county line.  Fifty-four homes were damaged by floodwaters of which ten were destroyed and another 27 incurred major damage.
The Withlacoochee River in Dade City crested at 16 feet, four feet above flood stage and flooded several homes including the Withlacoochee RV park and Canoe Rental.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding of 70 to 80 homes along a one mile strip of the Anclote River near Elfers in Pasco county where eight to nine inches of rain with isolated amounts of up to 14 inches fell in less than twelve hours.Floodwaters caused nearly one million dollars worth of damage to mechanical equipment at the Stearns Peat Company along U.S. Highway 98 near Dade City in eastern Pasco County.An estimated half million dollars worth of crops, mainly strawberry, were damaged throughout portions of eastern Pasco county by the heavy rains.  Property damage from in Pasco county from the flood was estimated at $2.95 million dollars.In Hillsborough county, eight homes in north Tampa lost electrical power due to the floodwaters caused by heavy rain of up to seven inches in less than twelve hours.  Also, several properties along an area from Nebraska Avenue and 22nd Street east to the University of South Florida along Fowler Avenue incurred water damage from the floodwaters.  These included twenty-two homes, 91 apartments and two mobile homes damaged by the floodwaters along low lying or areas near the Hillsborough River.  Damage in Hillsborough county from the flood was estimated at $1.5 million dollars.In Manatee county, several mobile homes were flooded at the Frog Creek Campground in Rubonia.  Damage in Manatee county from the flood was estimated at $50,000 dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty homes in the Mariner's Cove Mobile Home Park in Largo were flooded with water up to four feet deep from heavy rains.

The Manatee River at Myakka Head along State Road 64 crested at 13.8 feet on the 28th, over six and a half feet above the flood stage of seven feet.  Significant crop damage occurred, mainly tomatoes, from the floodwaters.
In Hillsborough county, heavy rains caused flood damage to 52 rental homes along and near the Alafia River in Riverview, which crested at 17.0 feet on the 14th.

In Hillsborough county, heavy rains cause the Little Manatee River to crest at 16.0 feet, five feet above the  flood stage of eleven feet, at U.S. Highway 301.  Several homes along 32nd to 33rd streets in Ruskin were damaged by floodwaters.
The Peace River along State Road 60 in Bartow crested at 8.5, one half foot above the flood stage of eight feet, on the 31st.

The Withlacoochee River rose to 15.8 feet on the 31st, which caused 29 homes and 60 properties along the river to flood.  Flood stage for the Withlacoochee River at Trilby was 12 feet.  Eight homes from Trilby to Lacoochee were destroyed and 20 more damaged by floodwaters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog that reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile occurred along the shores of Lake Hernando and along the U.S. Highway 41 corridor.
Thunderstorm winds caused minor shingle and roof damage to a few homes in Lakeland.  A few large trees and several power lines were downed near U.S. Highway 92 and State Road 37 in Lakeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding to homes along and near the Peace River along U.S. Highway 17 near the Hardee-De Soto county line.  The Horse Creek in Hardee county also overflowed it's banks and caused minor to moderate water damage to homes and businesses.  Damage in Hardee county from the flooding was estimated at $250,000.In Pinellas county, heavy rainfall caused flooding in two mobile home parks in Largo.  The heavy rainfall seriously damaged road infrastructure along Curlew Road from County Road 1 to Belcher Road. Flooding of homes and roads also occurred in the Tarpon Woods area along Brooker Creek in Pinellas county.  Damage in Pinellas county from the flood was estimated at $500,000.In Polk county, five to seven inches of rain fell from Lakeland south to Bartow and east to Auburndale and caused extensive road and infrastructure damage.  Most of the damage occurred in the Green Swamp, along Spirit Lake Road, Misty Lake Road, Thornhill Road and Red Hawk Drive where eight to ten homes were flooded with six to twelve inches of water with isolated pockets up to four feet deep.  Damage from the flood in Polk county was estimated at $750,000.In Sarasota county, six to eight inches of rain in less than twelve hours caused road and home flooding along Lockwood Ridge Road near Bahia Vista Street in Sarasota.  Damage from the flood in Sarasota county was estimated $80,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of heavy rain caused flooding and closure of up to eleven roads in northern Polk county.
Thunderstorm winds downed a few trees and several large branches along U.S. Highway 92 in Plant City.

The Hillsborough River at Hillsborough State Park crested at 14.2 feet, over four feet above the flood stage of ten feet, on the 28th.  Several mobile homes were flooded near U.S. Highway 301 and County Road 579.
The Peace River in Arcadia crested at 13.0 feet, two feet above flood stage, and flooded a few homes in Arcadia.

The Manatee River at Myakka Head along State Road 64 crested at 15.6 feet on the 14th, over eight and a half feet above the flood stage of seven feet.  A few homes in the flood plain and along the banks of the Manatee River received flood damage.
The Horse Creek crested at 14.4 feet, near two and a half feet above the flood stage of of twelve feet, on the 16th.  A few homes in the Hidden acres subdivision received minor flood damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Average rainfall amounts of five to eight inches with isolated amounts as high as twelve inches fell in less than twelve hours and caused flooding of homes, businesses, public facilities, roadways and rivers.  Most of the flooding occurred over a broad band from the Bradenton-Sarasota metropolitan area east to Wauchula in Hardee county and continued east to Sebring in northern Highlands county.The greatest flood damage occurred in Sarasota county where 178 properties were affected for a total of $2.3 million dollars.  Out of these properties damaged, six were destroyed, 36 had major damage and 136 had minor flood damage.In Manatee county, flooding caused $400,000 dollars worth of damage to fifteen single family dwellings and seven mobile homes.  Most of these dwellings were damaged by rising floodwaters of the Braden and Manatee Rivers.In De Soto county, flood damage totalled an estimated $380,000 dollars.  Floodwaters damaged six homes, three businesses and at least 25 vehicles.  Most of the damage occurred from rising floodwaters of the Peace River and Horse Creek.In Highlands county, flood damage of $100,000 was reported to vehicles along U.S. Highway 27 between Sebring and Avon Park.In Hardee county, flood damage estimates were $60,000 dollars.  Floodwaters damaged three mobile homes along the Peace River.

A tornado touched down near the intersection of Thonotasassa and Gallagher Roads and lifted near Forbes Road.  Two mobile homes were destroyed while thirteen others suffered minor damage.  One two story home suffered significant siding damage while a barn was flattened by the tornado.  Several large trees were snapped half way up the base by the tornadic wind.  Also, a doghouse was destroyed by the tornado.

High winds caused roof damage to a couple of homes generally along County Road 164 just northeast of Walnut Hill.  Several outbuildings suffered damage.  One barn was damaged and another under construction was blown down.  Trees and power lines were also blown down.  Several vehicles also suffered damage from the falling trees.  It appeared that microburst winds of 70 to 80 mph caused the damage.
A tornado briefly touched down and uprooted a few trees, twisted a couple of stop signs and downed a couple of power lines on Caladesi Island State Park.
Thunderstorm winds downed several power lines across Bradenton.
Thunderstorm winds severely damaged the roofs of several homes on Palisades Drive in the Town & Country area of western Hillsborough county.

A tornado touched down near the intersection of North 50th Street and Martin Luther King Boulevard.  The tornado downed nine 75 foot light poles at a commercial auto auction car lot.  The poles were sheared six feet above ground level and fell atop several parked vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved ashore causing minimal damage to a convenience store and a few mobile homes.

Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines in Kissimmee.

Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees in Deltona. Some trees fell on houses.
Two waterspouts moved ashore at Seagrove Beach along Walton County Road 30A and Seacrest Drive.  Eleven homes heavily damaged and one destroyed.

An F1 tornado touched down five miles southeast of Vernon near Greenhead on Highway 279.  Three barns, a pickup truck and a doublewide trailer were destroyed.  Two homes sustained roof damage.  Numerous trees were uprooted.

High winds caused minor damage to three homes along State Road 87 just northeast of Jay.  Trees and power lines were also blown down.  Several vehicles suffered damage from the fallen trees.  Two persons suffered minor injures.  The damage appeared to have been caused from a microburst of 70 to 80 mph.
Several trees were blown down near the University of West Florida.
Trees were blown down along State Road 87 in Milton.
Several trees were blown down near the community of Holt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying tornado initially touched down in New Smyrna Beach about 1 mile west of U.S. Highway 1 between Enterprise and Wayne Avenues. The tornado moved east northeast at about 50 mph damaging several homes and downing trees and power lines. It lifted just west of Highway 1 after passing Chisholm Elementary School. The tornado then struck New Smyrna Beach High School producing Fujita Scale F1 damage. As the tornado intensified to a strong F3 category, it passed thorough the Venetian VIllas subdivision and the Diamond Head Condominiums. A number of large well built homes were destroyed.  Many of the units in the 10 story Diamond Head Condominiums received major damage as high winds blew out exterior glass walls and blew furniture into the adjoining Indian River Lagoon. The tornado then crossed the Intracoastal Waterway and passed over the barrier island between Robinson Road and East Street where a number of large well built homes were almost completely destroyed. The tornado then moved over the Atlantic Ocean. In all 32 people were injured, six requiring hospitalisation. Thirty one homes were destroyed and 290 were damaged. Damage estimates were near 14 million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees near Lisbon.
F1 tornado destroyed one home five miles west of Blountstown.  One minor injury reported.

A fast moving F0 tornado first touched down along Interstate Highway 95 near S.R. 518. The tornado moved rapidly to the east northeast damaging road signs, snapping limbs off of trees and damaging the housing around an airconditioning system on the roof of a grocery store in Melbourne. The tornado continued east northeast and crossed the Indian River and Banana River lagoons and moved over Satellite Beach where it produced minimal damage to tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of five to seven inches caused localized flooding of roads and low lying areas from Ft Meade in Polk county south to Wauchula in Hardee county.  Six mobile homes received nearly $155,000 dollars worth of structural damage from flood waters in De Soto county.  Damage in Hardee and Polk counties was primarily vehicle flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Average rainfall amounts of five to eight inches with isolated amounts as high as twelve inches fell in less than twelve hours and caused flooding of homes, businesses, public facilities, roadways and rivers.  Most of the flooding occurred over a broad band from the Bradenton-Sarasota metropolitan area east to Wauchula in Hardee county and continued east to Sebring in northern Highlands county.The greatest flood damage occurred in Sarasota county where 178 properties were affected for a total of $2.3 million dollars.  Out of these properties damaged, six were destroyed, 36 had major damage and 136 had minor flood damage.In Manatee county, flooding caused $400,000 dollars worth of damage to fifteen single family dwellings and seven mobile homes.  Most of these dwellings were damaged by rising floodwaters of the Braden and Manatee Rivers.In De Soto county, flood damage totalled an estimated $380,000 dollars.  Floodwaters damaged six homes, three businesses and at least 25 vehicles.  Most of the damage occurred from rising floodwaters of the Peace River and Horse Creek.In Highlands county, flood damage of $100,000 was reported to vehicles along U.S. Highway 27 between Sebring and Avon Park.In Hardee county, flood damage estimates were $60,000 dollars.  Floodwaters damaged three mobile homes along the Peace River.

F0 tornado inflicted structural damage to Woolworths department store on US Highway 90 in Marianna.  Numerous 12" diameter trees snapped.
A short-lived tornado touched down near U.S. Highway 17 northeast of Ft. Ogden in rural southwest De Soto county and snapped a few power poles.
A small tornado blew the roof off a church, downed several power lines and uprooted three large trees on County Road 555, two miles north of County Road 630.

A tornado touched down near State Road 699 (Gulf Boulevard) and 6th Avenue, moved northeast and dissipated near the intersection of County Road 590 and Old Coachman Road.  The tornado left thousands of electrical customers without power, knocked down numerous trees, caused several roofs to collapse and shattered windows from Indian Rocks Beach northeast through Largo and primarily in Clearwater.Damage from the tornado totalled $4.5 million dollars.  From Indian Rocks Beach, through Largo to Clearwater, 190 buildings received damage from the tornado.  Four million dollars of the damage occurred to properties in Clearwater, $300,000 in Indian Rocks Beach, and $200,000 in Largo.The tornado caused major damage to 19 homes while another 135 suffered minor damage.  Sixteen apartment buildings incurred major damage while ten others suffered minor damage from the tornadic wind.  Also, eleven businesses and a high school gym in Clearwater suffered minor to moderate damage.
Gusts to 81 mph was reported at Patrick AFB. Trees were blown down in Mims.
Power lines were blown down in Titusville.

Lightning from the cirrus anvil of a distant thunderstorm struck and killed a nine year old child and injured two other children ages eleven and fifteen.  The children were standing along the sidelines of the Wildwood High School football practice field.  The nine year old male child died ten days after the strike.The nine year old child took the direct lightning hit and sustained major burn injuries to his forehead, chest, back and down the left side of his body to the soles of his feet.  The two older children's injuries from the lighting were minor.  The lightning struck nearly 35 minutes after a thunderstorm had passed over the field.

High winds, estimated at around 80 mph ,  damaged five mobile homes  just off of State Road 197 near Pine Meadows Loop Road.   The microburst destroyed one of the homes and caused minor damage to the other four.   A couple of vehicles were also damaged by flying debris.   No injuries were reported.  Some trees were also blown down.
Numerous public sightings of a small tornado moving through East Heritage subdivision near Hiland Park.  Minor roof damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thirty hour rainfall totals of eight to fourteen inches caused flooding of roads, highways, homes, commercial buildings, low-lying areas and rivers over much of Pinellas and Hillsborough counties.The heaviest rainfall and subsequent flooding occurred over the cities of Gulfport, Pinellas Park and St. Petersburg in southern Pinellas county.  In Hillsborough county, the heaviest rainfall and subsequent flooding occurred from Plant City and Brandon south to Ruskin and Sun City over eastern and southern portions of Hillsborough county.Damage in Hillsborough county from the flooding totalled $5.3 million dollars.  Out of 205 homes damaged from the flooding, 55 sustained major damage for a total of $1,611,000 dollars.  Six businesses suffered damage, two considered major, for a total of $1,030,000 dollars.  Three non-profit businesses also suffered flood damage which totalled $2,661,000 dollars.  No damage was reported in Pinellas county.In St. Petersburg, a thirteen year old female was swept into a storm drain while playing in floodwaters at a city park on 36th Avenue N. and Eighth Street at 7 pm est.  She was dragged into raging waters underground for two blocks before she emerged and was rescued by firefighters.  She died from the flood injuries at 9 am est on 9/28/97.In St. Petersburg, floodwaters carried a 23 year old woman twelve blocks underground in a drain before she was dumped into a retention pond with only minor injuries around 3 pm est.  The female was playing at Booker Creek under a wooden bridge with two children on the 2100 block of Ninth Avenue N., one-eighth mile west of I-275, when she lost her footing and was carried off by the fast moving flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of four to six inches caused localized flooding of roads, streams and low-lying areas over a broad area of Southwest and South Central Florida.
Two F1 tornados reported along Highway 277 near Vernon.  Several homes suffered considerable roof damage.  A  mobile home was damaged between Vernon and Wausau on Highway 79.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees south of St. Cloud.  Street flooding was also reported.
Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines in Lake Buena Vista.
Urban flooding caused rug damage to a few homes and vehicles in low lying areas west of U.S. Highway 19 from Holiday north to New Port Richey.
Thunderstorm winds caused minor roof shingle and lanai damage to 42 structures near the intersection of Del Prado Boulevard and SE 40th Street in Cape Coral.
An F1 tornado lifted a roof off a barn four miles west of Malone.  Many large trees toppled and some fences destroyed.
A small F0 tornado observed from Heritage Woods Road to the St. Andrew Marina.  No damage reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of two to three inches in a little over an hour, caused  street flooding and high water  in some buildings in the Fort Walton Beach area.  The Okaloosa Island fire department  reported several inches of water in their building.  The Emergency Operations Center in Fort Walton also had several inches of water in their building.  The water subsided shortly after the heavy rains stopped.
Two pine trees were uprooted just east of Milton.  Part of a fence was blown down and a couple of windows were blown out of a motor home.
Several large limbs were blown down in the Fort Walton Beach area.
Heavy rains in the west part of Crestview caused flooding along Old Antioch Road.  A bridge across the road had to be closed for almost an hour due to high water.

Heavy rains produced widespread flooding and ponding of water across Northeast Florida. Amounts ranged from 3 to 7.5 inches. Numerous streets and roads were closed with water 20 feet deep in some spots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail continued to fall along U S Highway 90 from the storm that dropped hail in the Seminole Alabama community.  Hail fell from the Alabama and Florida state line east along Highway 90 to I-10.  A couple of vehicles had their windshields cracked by the large hail.
Washington County Sheriff observed nickel size hail in Chipley.
Severe thunderstorm winds blew down trees and some power lines near the Liberty-Gadsden county line.
Dime size hail observed in southeastern Leon County at the Florida Warning Point headquarters.  Street flooding reported in west and central portions of Tallahassee.
Dime size hail in eastern Jackson County near Grand Ridge.

Several trees and power lines were blown down around the Baker community.

Two waterspouts were reported in Blackwater Bay, south of Bagdad.  The waterspouts dissipated over the bay.
Storm spotters and Jackson County Sheriff reported dime size hail in Marianna.
